text: Looking back: "I {{c1::vividly remember}} visiting the Andes; it was an {{c2::eye-opening experience}} that changed how I saw nature."
explanation: Vividly remember means to recall with clear, bright, and detailed memory. **Eye-opening experience** is an event that teaches you something surprising or changes your perspective.
usage:
Pattern: vividly remember [action/event] / eye-opening experience
  • I vividly remember my first coding project.
  • Working in tech support was an eye-opening experience.
spanish: Mirando hacia atrás: "Recuerdo vívidamente haber visitado los Andes; fue una experiencia reveladora que cambió mi forma de ver la naturaleza".

text: Recalling his last job: "I was {{c1::snowed under}} with tickets, and when the server crashed, I was at my {{c2::wit's end}}."
explanation: Snowed under is an idiom meaning overwhelmed with work. **At one's wit's end** is an idiom meaning completely stressed out and not knowing what to do next.
usage:
Pattern: be snowed under with [work] / at my wit's end
  • I'm completely snowed under with homework this semester.
  • I was at my wit's end trying to solve the connection bug.
spanish: Recordando su último trabajo: "Estaba inundado de tickets, y cuando el servidor se cayó, estaba al borde de la locura (no sabía qué hacer)".

text: Telling the story: "We crossed paths {{c1::out of the blue}} in Berlin, and we {{c2::hit it off}} immediately."
explanation: Out of the blue is an idiom meaning completely unexpected. **Hit it off** is a phrasal verb meaning to quickly become friendly or connect with someone.
usage:
Pattern: cross paths out of the blue / hit it off with [someone]
  • I ran into my former colleague out of the blue.
  • We hit it off during our first meeting at the conference.
spanish: Contando la historia: "Nos cruzamos por casualidad (de la nada) en Berlín, y congeniamos de inmediato".

text: Relating the emergency: "I got a {{c1::minor gash}} on my arm, but the doctor bandaged it and issued a {{c2::medical discharge}} within an hour."
explanation: Gash is a long, deep cut or wound in the skin. **Medical discharge** is the official release of a patient from medical care.
usage:
Pattern: gash on [body part] / receive a medical discharge
  • He had a deep gash on his knee after falling.
  • The hospital prepared his medical discharge papers.
spanish: Relatando la emergencia: "Me hice un corte menor en el brazo, pero el médico lo vendó y emitió un alta médica en una hora".

text: Reflecting on the project: "In {{c1::hindsight}}, the failed launch was a blessing in disguise because our {{c2::key takeaway}} was the need for automated tests."
explanation: In hindsight means looking back at an event in the past. **Blessing in disguise** is an idiom meaning something bad that turns out to have good results. **Takeaway** is the key lesson learned.
usage:
Pattern: in hindsight, [situation] was a blessing in disguise / key takeaway from [event]
  • In hindsight, losing that client helped us focus on better partners.
  • The key takeaway from the security audit was to rotate keys immediately.
spanish: Reflexionando sobre el proyecto: "En retrospectiva, el lanzamiento fallido fue una bendición disfrazada porque nuestro principal aprendizaje fue la necesidad de pruebas automatizadas".

text: To make a long story short, we ended up missing our flight and had to spend the night at the {{c1::airport terminal}}.
explanation: To 'make a long story short' is a very common idiom used to skip minor details and summarize the final outcome of an event quickly.
usage:
Pattern: to make a long story short / long story short
  • Long story short, I got the job.
  • To make a long story short, we had to walk home in the rain.
spanish: Para no hacerles el cuento largo (resumiendo), terminamos perdiendo nuestro vuelo y tuvimos que pasar la noche en la terminal del aeropuerto.

text: Believe it or not, she actually won the lottery but decided to donate all the winnings to {{c1::charity}}.
explanation: 'Believe it or not' is a conversational expression used to introduce a surprising, remarkable, or unexpected fact that is nevertheless true.
usage:
Pattern: believe it or not / surprising fact
  • Believe it or not, I've never eaten sushi.
  • He completed the marathon in two hours, believe it or not.
spanish: Lo creas o no, ella realmente ganó la lotería pero decidió donar todas las ganancias a la caridad.

text: Out of the blue, my childhood friend called me after ten years of no contact, which was a pleasant {{c1::surprise}}.
explanation: 'Out of the blue' is an idiom meaning completely unexpectedly, suddenly, or without any warning.
usage:
Pattern: out of the blue / suddenly
  • She quit her job out of the blue.
  • He showed up at my house out of the blue.
spanish: De la nada (inesperadamente), mi amigo de la infancia me llamó después de diez años sin contacto, lo cual fue una grata sorpresa.

text: To cut to the chase, we need to approve the proposal today if we want to meet our launch {{c1::deadline}}.
explanation: To 'cut to the chase' is an idiom meaning to stop talking about minor details and address the most important part of a matter directly.
usage:
Pattern: cut to the chase / get straight to point
  • Let's cut to the chase: how much will this cost?
  • The director cut to the chase and announced the layoffs.
spanish: Para ir al grano (resumiendo), necesitamos aprobar la propuesta hoy si queremos cumplir con nuestra fecha límite de lanzamiento.

text: As luck would have it, I found my lost keys right when I was about to call a locksmith to {{c1::replace them}}.
explanation: 'As luck would have it' is an idiom meaning by chance or fortune (can be used for both good and bad luck).
usage:
Pattern: as luck would have it / fortunately / unfortunately
  • As luck would have it, our train was delayed by two hours.
  • I forgot my umbrella, but as luck would have it, it didn't rain.
spanish: Como quiso la suerte (por suerte), encontré mis llaves perdidas justo cuando estaba a punto de llamar a un cerrajero para reemplazarlas.
